license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.notice: Event descriptions are paraphrased summaries written for this dataset. Source links point to authoritative references.

entries.title: Malware or unwanted software detected
entries.details: Generated when Windows Defender detects malware or potentially unwanted software. Key fields: Threat Name, Category, Severity Level, Detection Source (real-time, on-access, scheduled scan, etc.), Process Name (the process associated with the detection), and Path (the file or object flagged). Correlate Threat Name against the detection timeline to identify what the attacker was running when Defender fired. Process Name is especially valuable — detections attributed to LOLBins (e.g., powershell.exe, mshta.exe, wscript.exe) are higher priority than detections in temp directories.

investigationPivots: Pivot on Process Name to identify what was executing the detected payload
Correlate Path with file creation events to determine how the file arrived on the system
Detection Source of 'Real-Time Protection' means the threat was caught during active execution; 'Scheduled Scan' may indicate an older dormant file
Correlate timestamp with Security EID 4688 to find related process activity
commonFalsePositives: Security research tools or red team utilities flagged as PUA/PUP
Packed or obfuscated legitimate software triggering heuristic detections

entries.title: Antivirus took action to protect machine from malware
entries.summary: Windows Defender took a remediation action on a detected threat.
entries.details: Generated when Windows Defender performs a remediation action following a detection (EID 1116). Key fields: Threat Name, Action (Quarantine, Remove, Allow, Block, Clean), Action Status (success or failure), and ThreatID. Action Status of failure means the threat was detected but not successfully remediated — the file or process may still be active. Correlate ThreatID with EID 1116 to link detection and response. An action of 'Allow' or 'No Action Taken' may indicate the threat was excluded or the user overrode the detection.

investigationPivots: Action Status failure means the file or process may still be present — escalate immediately
Action of 'Allow' or 'No Action Taken' warrants investigation into whether an exclusion was in place
Correlate ThreatID with EID 1116 to get the full detection context including file path and process
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate software quarantined due to false positive detection
Security tooling excluded from scanning triggering no-action outcomes

entries.title: Attack Surface Reduction rule triggered in block mode
entries.summary: An ASR rule blocked a potentially malicious operation.
entries.details: Generated when an Attack Surface Reduction (ASR) rule fires in block mode and prevents an operation. Key fields: the rule GUID (identifies which ASR rule triggered), Process Name (the process that attempted the blocked operation), and Path (the target file or object). Each ASR rule GUID maps to a specific rule such as 'Block Office applications from creating executable content' or 'Block credential stealing from Windows LSASS'. An ASR block during an incident is a high-confidence signal that an attacker technique was attempted and stopped.

investigationPivots: Map the rule GUID to a specific ASR rule using Microsoft's ASR rule reference to understand what technique was blocked
Process Name reveals what attempted the blocked operation — flag Office apps, scripting engines, or LOLBins
Correlate timestamp and process with Security EID 4688 to build the full execution chain
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate Office macros or scripts blocked by overly broad ASR rules
Developer tools triggering code-execution ASR rules

entries.title: Attack Surface Reduction rule triggered in audit mode
entries.summary: An ASR rule would have blocked an operation but is in audit mode.
entries.details: Generated when an ASR rule fires in audit mode — the operation was allowed to proceed but would have been blocked in enforcement mode. Key fields are identical to EID 1121: rule GUID, Process Name, and Path. Audit mode events are valuable during incident investigations to identify attacker techniques that were attempted but not blocked, particularly in environments where ASR rules have not yet been moved to enforcement. Treat audit events from known-bad processes with the same urgency as block events.

investigationPivots: Audit mode means the operation succeeded — treat as executed attacker activity, not just an attempt
Same pivot fields as EID 1121: rule GUID, Process Name, Path
Use audit events to identify which ASR rules should be promoted to block mode
commonFalsePositives: Same as EID 1121 — audit mode means no blocking occurred, so false positive impact is limited to alert noise

entries.title: Real-time protection is disabled
entries.summary: Windows Defender real-time protection was turned off.
entries.details: Generated when Windows Defender real-time protection is disabled. This is a high-priority alert during an incident — disabling real-time protection is a common attacker technique to prevent detection of subsequent payloads. Correlate the timestamp with process creation and logon events to identify what account or process disabled protection. Legitimate causes include Group Policy-managed AV exclusions, third-party AV coexistence, and IT administrative actions; these should be validated against change management records.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

investigationPivots: Correlate timestamp with Security EID 4688 and 4624 to identify what process or account made the change
Check whether the change corresponds to a known Group Policy or administrative action
Real-time protection disabled immediately before a malware detection or payload execution is a strong compromise indicator
commonFalsePositives: IT administrators temporarily disabling protection for maintenance or software installation
Third-party AV products disabling Defender as part of coexistence management
Group Policy enforcing a disabled state on managed endpoints

entries.title: Antimalware platform configuration changed
entries.summary: A Windows Defender configuration setting was modified.
entries.details: Generated when any Windows Defender configuration setting changes. Key field: the old and new configuration values, recorded in the format 'HKLM\...\value = old value -> new value'. Configuration changes of particular interest include additions to ExclusionPath, ExclusionProcess, or ExclusionExtension (attackers add exclusions to prevent detection of their tools), and changes to DisableRealtimeMonitoring, DisableBehaviorMonitoring, or DisableIOAVProtection. Exclusion additions should be correlated against known software deployments.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

investigationPivots: Changes to ExclusionPath or ExclusionProcess are the highest-priority — they may be hiding attacker tooling
Correlate the changed value with the file or directory path to determine what is being excluded
Correlate timestamp with Security EID 4688 and 4624 to identify what made the configuration change
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate software installers adding Defender exclusions during setup
Group Policy or MDM pushing exclusion policies to managed endpoints
